lem:strict-overlap-sanity-corrected.proof_tex:
If \(\lambda<\eta_-\) and \(e(X)\ge \eta_-\) almost surely, then \(e(X)>\lambda\) almost surely. Hence
\[
e_\lambda(X)=\max\{e(X),\lambda\}=e(X)
\]
almost surely, and
\[
\pi(\lambda)=P\{e(X)\le \lambda\}=0.
\]
Substituting these identities into `def:exact-frontier-functional` gives
\[
U_n(\lambda)=n^{-1/2}E[e(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}
+n^{-1/2}(\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en})
+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
This is the corrected strict-overlap simplification.

defs.def:exact-frontier-functional: U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + n^(-1/2) (lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + pi(lambda)^(1/2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

lem:absolute-risk-two-point.proof_tex:
Let \(\theta_P=\theta(P)\), \(\theta_Q=\theta(Q)\), and \(\Delta=|\theta_P-\theta_Q|\). Without loss of generality, \(\theta_Q>\theta_P\). Consider the test
\[
\phi=\mathbf 1\{T\ge (\theta_P+\theta_Q)/2\}.
\]
If \(\phi=1\), then \(|T-\theta_P|\ge \Delta/2\). If \(\phi=0\), then \(|T-\theta_Q|\ge \Delta/2\). Therefore
\[
E_P|T-\theta_P|\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}P_P(\phi=1),
\qquad
E_Q|T-\theta_Q|\ge \frac{\Delta}{2}P_Q(\phi=0).
\]
Since \(\max\{a,b\}\ge (a+b)/2\),
\[
\max\{E_P|T-\theta_P|,E_Q|T-\theta_Q|\}
\ge \frac{\Delta}{4}\{P_P(\phi=1)+P_Q(\phi=0)\}.
\]
Now use the standard testing bound
\[
P_P(\phi=1)+P_Q(\phi=0)\ge 1-\operatorname{TV}(P,Q).
\]
Combining the last two displays yields
\[
\max\{E_P|T-\theta_P|,E_Q|T-\theta_Q|\}
\ge \frac{\Delta}{4}\bigl(1-\operatorname{TV}(P,Q)\bigr).
\]
This is the claim.

snapshot.stmt: For any two laws P and Q on the sample space and any estimator T of a scalar parameter theta, max{E_P|T-theta(P)|,E_Q|T-theta(Q)|} >= |theta(P)-theta(Q)|/4 * (1-TV(P,Q)).

lem:oracle-clipped-score-unbiased.proof_tex:
Fix a deterministic $\lambda \in \Lambda_n$. Under `ass:consistency` and `ass:ignorability`, for each treatment arm $d\in\{0,1\}$,
+\[
+g_d(X)=E[Y\mid D=d,X]=E[Y(d)\mid X]
+\]
+almost surely: consistency identifies $Y$ with $Y(d)$ on the event $\{D=d\}$, and ignorability removes the conditioning on $D$. Therefore
+\[
+\tau=E[Y(1)-Y(0)]=E\bigl[E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X]\bigr]=E[g_1(X)-g_0(X)].
+\]
+Now condition on $X$. Since $e_\lambda(X)$ is a function of $X$ alone,
+\[
+E\!\left[\frac{D\{Y-g_1(X)\}}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigm|X\right]
+=\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}E\!\left[D\,E\{Y-g_1(X)\mid D,X\}\Bigm|X\right]=0,
+\]
+because $E[Y-g_1(X)\mid D=1,X]=0$ by the definition of $g_1$. Likewise,
+\[
+E\!\left[\frac{(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}}{1-e(X)}\Bigm|X\right]
+=\frac{1}{1-e(X)}E\!\left[(1-D)E\{Y-g_0(X)\mid D,X\}\Bigm|X\right]=0,
+\]
+because $E[Y-g_0(X)\mid D=0,X]=0$. Therefore
+\[
+E[\psi_\lambda(O;g_0,g_1,e)\mid X]=g_1(X)-g_0(X).
+\]
+Taking expectations and using the previous display for $\tau$ gives
+\[
+E_P[\psi_\lambda(O;g_0,g_1,e)]=E[g_1(X)-g_0(X)]=\tau.
+\]
+This proves the unbiasedness claim.

snapshot.stmt: For every deterministic lambda in Lambda_n and every law P satisfying ass:consistency and ass:ignorability, E_P[psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e)] = tau.

defs.def:oracle-clipped-score: psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e) = g_1(X) - g_0(X) + D (Y - g_1(X)) / e_lambda(X) - (1 - D) (Y - g_0(X)) / (1 - e(X))

assumptions.ass:consistency: Y = D Y(1) + (1 - D) Y(0) almost surely.
assumptions.ass:ignorability: (Y(1), Y(0)) is independent of D conditional on X.

lem:feasible-score-drift-decomposition.proof_tex:
Continue to fix $\lambda \in \Lambda_n$ and condition on the training folds, so the fitted nuisance functions are deterministic for the validation observation. Introduce
+\[
+\delta_1=\hat g_{1n}-g_1,\qquad \delta_0=\hat g_{0n}-g_0,\qquad \hat e_{+,n}(x)=\min\{\hat e_n(x),1-\eta_+/2\}.
+\]
+By the definitions of `def:oracle-clipped-score`, `def:estimated-clipped-propensity`, and `def:plugin-score`,
+\[
+\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda
+=\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{D}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)
+-\delta_0(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\Bigr)
++D\{Y-g_1(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)
+-(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}-\frac{1}{1-e(X)}\Bigr).
+\]
+The last two summands have conditional mean zero, because $E[Y-g_1(X)\mid D=1,X]=0$ and $E[Y-g_0(X)\mid D=0,X]=0$. Hence
+\[
+P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)
+=P\!\left[\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)\right]
+-P\!\left[\delta_0(X)\frac{e(X)-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\right].
+\]
+
+We bound the treated term by splitting according to whether the true propensity falls below the clipping level.
+
+On the event $\{e(X)\le \lambda\}$, since $\hat e_\lambda(X)\ge \lambda\ge e(X)$, we have
+\[
+0\le \frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\le 1,
+\]
+so $|1-e(X)/\hat e_\lambda(X)|\le 1$. Therefore Cauchy-Schwarz gives
+\[
+\left|P\!\left[\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)\mathbf 1\{e(X)\le \lambda\}\right]\right|
+\le P(e(X)\le \lambda)^{1/2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}
+=\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+
+On the event $\{e(X)>\lambda\}$, we have $e_\lambda(X)=e(X)$. Hence
+\[
+1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}=\frac{\hat e_\lambda(X)-e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}.
+\]
+Since $\hat e_\lambda(X)\ge \lambda$ pointwise,
+\[
+\left|1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\right|\le \lambda^{-1}|\hat e_\lambda(X)-e(X)|.
+\]
+The map $u\mapsto \min\{1-\eta_+/2,\max\{u,\lambda\}\}$ is $1$-Lipschitz. Because it sends $e$ to $e_\lambda$ and $e_\lambda=e$ on $\{e>\lambda\}$,
+\[
+|\hat e_\lambda(X)-e(X)|\le |\hat e_n(X)-e(X)|
+\qquad\text{on }\{e(X)>\lambda\}.
+\]
+Therefore
+\[
+\left|P\!\left[\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{e(X)}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr)\mathbf 1\{e(X)>\lambda\}\right]\right|
+\le \lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+
+For the control term, `ass:right-overlap` gives $e(X)\le 1-\eta_+$ almost surely, while by definition $\hat e_{+,n}(X)\le 1-\eta_+/2$. Hence
+\[
+1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)\ge \eta_+/2.
+\]
+Also the map $u\mapsto \min\{u,1-\eta_+/2\}$ is $1$-Lipschitz and fixes $e(X)$ because $e(X)\le 1-\eta_+<1-\eta_+/2$. Thus
+\[
+|e(X)-\hat e_{+,n}(X)|\le |e(X)-\hat e_n(X)|.
+\]
+Combining the last two displays,
+\[
+\left|\frac{e(X)-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\right|
+\le \frac{2}{\eta_+}|\hat e_n(X)-e(X)|.
+\]
+Another application of Cauchy-Schwarz gives
+\[
+\left|P\!\left[\delta_0(X)\frac{e(X)-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\right]\right|
+\le \frac{2}{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+
+Adding the bounds for the three pieces proves
+\[
+|P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)|
+\le \pi(\lambda)^{1/2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\frac{2}{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+This is the claimed drift decomposition.

lem:feasible-score-fluctuation-bound.proof_tex:
Again condition on the training folds. Then the fitted nuisances are fixed and independent of the validation sample, so
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda=\mathbb G_n\hat\psi_\lambda,
+\qquad
+\mathbb G_n f = n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n\{f(O_i)-Pf\}.
+\]
+Write the feasible score as
+\[
+\hat\psi_\lambda=\psi_\lambda+A_1-A_0+B_1-B_0,
+\]
+where
+\[
+A_1(O)=\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{D}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),
+\qquad
+A_0(O)=\delta_0(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\Bigr),
+\]
+\[
+B_1(O)=D\{Y-g_1(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),
+\qquad
+B_0(O)=(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}-\frac{1}{1-e(X)}\Bigr).
+\]
+Each centered summand has conditional mean zero. Therefore it suffices to bound the conditional second moment of each piece and apply Chebyshev's inequality term by term.
+
+For the oracle score, the treated residual component satisfies
+\[
+P\left[\frac{D\{Y-g_1(X)\}^2}{e_\lambda(X)^2}\right]
+=E\left[\frac{e(X)E[(Y-g_1(X))^2\mid D=1,X]}{e_\lambda(X)^2}\right]
+\le \sigma_1^2 E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}],
+\]
+because $e(X)\le e_\lambda(X)$ and `ass:resid-treated` bounds the conditional treated residual variance by $\sigma_1^2$. For the control residual component,
+\[
+P\left[\frac{(1-D)\{Y-g_0(X)\}^2}{(1-e(X))^2}\right]
+=E\left[\frac{(1-e(X))E[(Y-g_0(X))^2\mid D=0,X]}{(1-e(X))^2}\right]
+\le \sigma_0^2\eta_+^{-1},
+\]
+because `ass:right-overlap` gives $1-e(X)\ge \eta_+$ and `ass:resid-control` bounds the conditional control residual variance by $\sigma_0^2$. The regression part $g_1(X)-g_0(X)$ is square-integrable because `ass:estimable-g1` and `ass:estimable-g0` are $L_2(P_X)$ estimability statements. Thus
+\[
+P[\psi_\lambda(O;g_0,g_1,e)^2]\le C_P\{1+E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\}
+\]
+for a finite constant $C_P$ depending only on $P$, $\sigma_1^2$, $\sigma_0^2$, and $\eta_+$. Since $e_\lambda(X)\le 1$, we also have $E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\ge 1$, so the previous display simplifies to
+\[
+P[\psi_\lambda^2]\le C_P' E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}].
+\]
+Therefore
+\[
+\mathbb G_n\psi_\lambda=O_P\bigl(n^{-1/2}E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}\bigr).
+\]
+
+For $A_1$, since $\hat e_\lambda(X)\ge \lambda$,
+\[
+\left|1-\frac{D}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\right|\le 1+\lambda^{-1}\le 2\lambda^{-1}.
+\]
+Hence
+\[
+P[A_1^2]\le 4\lambda^{-2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}^2,
+\qquad
+\mathbb G_nA_1=O_P\bigl(n^{-1/2}\lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\bigr).
+\]
+
+For $A_0$, because $1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)\ge \eta_+/2$,
+\[
+\left|1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\right|\le 1+\frac{2}{\eta_+}.
+\]
+Therefore
+\[
+P[A_0^2]\le C_{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}^2,
+\qquad
+\mathbb G_nA_0=O_P\bigl(n^{-1/2}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\bigr).
+\]
+
+For $B_1$, the map $u\mapsto 1/u$ is $\lambda^{-2}$-Lipschitz on $[\lambda,1]$, so
+\[

assumptions.ass:iid: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:resid-treated: sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_1(X))^2 | D = 1, X = x] <= sigma_1_sq for some finite sigma_1_sq.

Again condition on the training folds. Then the fitted nuisances are fixed and independent of the validation sample, so
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda=\mathbb G_n\hat\psi_\lambda,
+\qquad
+\mathbb G_n f = n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n\{f(O_i)-Pf\}.
+\]
+Write the feasible score as
+\[
+\hat\psi_\lambda=\psi_\lambda+A_1-A_0+B_1-B_0,
+\]
+where
+\[
+A_1(O)=\delta_1(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{D}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),
+\qquad
+A_0(O)=\delta_0(X)\Bigl(1-\frac{1-D}{1-\hat e_{+,n}(X)}\Bigr),
+\]
+\[
+B_1(O)=D\{Y-g_1(X)\}\Bigl(\frac{1}{\hat e_\lambda(X)}-\frac{1}{e_\lambda(X)}\Bigr),
+\qquad

+\]

+=E\left[\frac{(1-e(X))E[(Y-g_0(X))^2\mid D=0,X]}{(1-e(X))^2}\right]
+\le \sigma_0^2\eta_+^{-1},

+\]

lem:frontier-rate-specialization.proof_tex:
Specialize the nuisance-rate sequences to
\[
r_{0n}=n^{-a_0},\qquad r_{1n}=n^{-a_1},\qquad r_{en}=n^{-a_e}.
\]
For $t\ge 0$, let $m_\kappa(t)$ denote the pointwise minimum appearing in Definition `def:phase-exponent`.

First suppose $\kappa
eq 1$. If $0<\kappa<1$ and $\lambda=\lambda_0n^{-t}$, Definition `def:frontier-functional` gives
\[
 egin{aligned}
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})={}&\lambda_0^{-(1-\kappa)/2}n^{-\{1/2-t(1-\kappa)/2\}}+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{1/2+a_1-t\}}+n^{-(1/2+a_0)}\
&+\lambda_0^{-3/2}n^{-\{1/2+a_e-3t/2\}}+n^{-(1/2+a_e)}+\lambda_0^{\kappa/2}n^{-\{a_1+t\kappa/2\}}\
&+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{a_1+a_e-t\}}+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
The exponents $1/2+a_0$ and $1/2+a_e$ are strictly larger than $1/2$, while the first exponent is at most $1/2$; hence those two omitted empirical-process terms can never determine the minimum rate. Therefore there exist constants $0<c\le C<\infty$, depending only on $(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa,\lambda_0)$, such that
\[
c\,n^{-m_\kappa(t)}\le R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})\le C\,n^{-m_\kappa(t)}\qquad (t\ge 0).
\]
Exactly the same conclusion holds when $\kappa>1$, because then the first term is $n^{-1/2}$ and the two omitted exponents $1/2+a_0$ and $1/2+a_e$ are again strictly larger than $1/2$.

The map $t\mapsto m_\kappa(t)$ is continuous and piecewise affine on $[0,\infty)$, and it satisfies
\[
m_\kappa(t)\le a_1+a_e-t,
\]
so $m_\kappa(t)	o-\infty$ as $t	o\infty$. Hence the supremum in `def:phase-exponent` is attained at some finite $t_\star\ge 0$. Since $ ar t_n	o\infty$, we have $t_\star\le  ar t_n$ for all large $n$, so $\lambda_0n^{-t_\star}\in\Lambda_n$. The upper bound at $t_\star$ gives
\[
R_n^*\le R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t_\star})\le Cn^{-m_\kappa(t_\star)}=Cn^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Conversely, every admissible grid point has the form $\lambda_0n^{-t}$ with $0\le t\le  ar t_n$, and for such $t$ we have $m_\kappa(t)\le 
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)$. Therefore
\[
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})\ge c n^{-m_\kappa(t)}\ge c n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Taking the infimum over $\Lambda_n$ yields
\[
c n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}\le R_n^*\le C n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
This proves the asserted power rate when $\kappa
eq 1$.

Now suppose $\kappa=1$, and write
\[
m_1(t)=\min\!\left\{ rac12,\ a_1+ rac t2,\  rac12+a_1-t,\  rac12+a_e- rac{3t}{2},\ a_1+a_e-t,\ a_0+a_eight\}.
\]
If $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2$, then $m_1(t)<1/2$ for every $t\ge 0$; otherwise some $t$ would achieve $m_1(t)=1/2$, contradicting the definition of $
u$. For $\lambda=\lambda_0n^{-t}$, Definition `def:frontier-functional` gives
\[
 egin{aligned}
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})={}&n^{-1/2}(1+t\log n)^{1/2}+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{1/2+a_1-t\}}+n^{-(1/2+a_0)}\
&+\lambda_0^{-3/2}n^{-\{1/2+a_e-3t/2\}}+n^{-(1/2+a_e)}+\lambda_0^{1/2}n^{-\{a_1+t/2\}}\
&+\lambda_0^{-1}n^{-\{a_1+a_e-t\}}+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
The polynomial part is again comparable to $n^{-m_1(t)}$, with constants depending only on $(a_0,a_1,a_e,\lambda_0)$. Because $m_1(t)<1/2$, the variance term $n^{-1/2}(1+t\log n)^{1/2}$ is $o(n^{-m_1(t)})$ for each fixed $t$. Repeating the same maximizer argument as above therefore yields constants $0<c\le C<\infty$ such that
\[
c n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}\le R_n^*\le C n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}
\]
whenever $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2$.

Assume next that $\kappa=1$ and $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)=1/2$. If $t=0$ attains the supremum in `def:phase-exponent`, then $m_1(0)=1/2$. Hence every polynomial term in $R_n(\lambda_0)$ has exponent at least $1/2$, while the variance term equals $n^{-1/2}$. Consequently
\[
R_n(\lambda_0)=O(n^{-1/2}),\qquad R_n^*\le R_n(\lambda_0)=O(n^{-1/2}).
\]
On the other hand, for every $\lambda\in\Lambda_n$, $v_1(\lambda)^2=1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)\ge 1$, so the first summand in `def:frontier-functional` implies
\[
R_n(\lambda)\ge n^{-1/2}.
\]
Taking the infimum over $\lambda$ gives $R_n^*\ge n^{-1/2}$. Hence
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-1/2}.
\]

Finally, assume that $\kappa=1$, $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)=1/2$, and every maximizer of $m_1$ is strictly positive. Since $m_1(t)\le a_1+a_e-t$, all maximizers lie in the compact interval $[0,a_1+a_e]$. Let
\[
M=\{t\in[0,a_1+a_e]:m_1(t)=1/2\}.
\]
The set $M$ is nonempty and closed, and by assumption $0
otin M$. Therefore $\delta:=\min M>0$. Because $m_1$ is continuous and does not reach $1/2$ on $[0,\delta/2]$, there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that
\[
m_1(t)\le 1/2-\varepsilon\qquad	ext{for all }0\le t\le \delta/2.
\]
For such $t$, the polynomial part of $R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})$ is bounded below by $c n^{-m_1(t)}$, so
\[
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})\ge c n^{-1/2+\varepsilon}.
\]
If instead $t\ge \delta/2$, then the variance term yields
\[
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})\ge n^{-1/2}(1+t\log n)^{1/2}\ge c_\delta n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
Because $n^{\varepsilon}/(\log n)^{1/2}	o\infty$, the lower bound $c n^{-1/2+\varepsilon}$ from the first regime is also at least $c' n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}$ for all large $n$. Hence there is a constant $c_{\log}>0$ such that
\[
R_n(\lambda)\ge c_{\log} n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}\qquad	ext{for every }\lambda\in\Lambda_n,
\]
so $R_n^*\ge c_{\log} n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}$. For the matching upper bound, choose any maximizer $t_\star\in M$. Then $t_\star>0$ is fixed and $m_1(t_\star)=1/2$, so the polynomial part of $R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t_\star})$ is $O(n^{-1/2})$, while the variance term is $O(n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2})$. Therefore
\[
R_n^*\le R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t_\star})\le C_{\log} n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
Combining the last two displays gives
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
This completes all four rate regimes.

If kappa = 1 and nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1) < 1/2, then R_n^* is of order n^(-nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)). u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}
\]
whenever $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2$.

Assume next that $\kappa=1$ and $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)=1/2$. If $t=0$ attains the supremum in `def:phase-exponent`, then $m_1(0)=1/2$. Hence every polynomial term in $R_n(\lambda_0)$ has exponent at least $1/2$, while the variance term equals $n^{-1/2}$. Consequently
\[
R_n(\lambda_0)=O(n^{-1/2}),\qquad R_n^*\le R_n(\lambda_0)=O(n^{-1/2}).
\]
On the other hand, for every $\lambda\in\Lambda_n$, $v_1(\lambda)^2=1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)\ge 1$, so the first summand in `def:frontier-functional` implies
\[
R_n(\lambda)\ge n^{-1/2}.
\]
Taking the infimum over $\lambda$ gives $R_n^*\ge n^{-1/2}$. Hence
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-1/2}.
\]

Finally, assume that $\kappa=1$, $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)=1/2$, and every maximizer of $m_1$ is strictly positive. Since $m_1(t)\le a_1+a_e-t$, all maximizers lie in the compact interval $[0,a_1+a_e]$. Let
\[
M=\{t\in[0,a_1+a_e]:m_1(t)=1/2\}.
\]
The set $M$ is nonempty and closed, and by assumption $0
otin M$. Therefore $\delta:=\min M>0$. Because $m_1$ is continuous and does not reach $1/2$ on $[0,\delta/2]$, there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that
\[
m_1(t)\le 1/2-\varepsilon\qquad	ext{for all }0\le t\le \delta/2.
\]
For such $t$, the polynomial part of $R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})$ is bounded below by $c n^{-m_1(t)}$, so
\[
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})\ge c n^{-1/2+\varepsilon}.
\]
If instead $t\ge \delta/2$, then the variance term yields
\[
R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t})\ge n^{-1/2}(1+t\log n)^{1/2}\ge c_\delta n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
Because $n^{\varepsilon}/(\log n)^{1/2}	o\infty$, the lower bound $c n^{-1/2+\varepsilon}$ from the first regime is also at least $c' n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}$ for all large $n$. Hence there is a constant $c_{\log}>0$ such that
\[
R_n(\lambda)\ge c_{\log} n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}\qquad	ext{for every }\lambda\in\Lambda_n,
\]
so $R_n^*\ge c_{\log} n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}$. For the matching upper bound, choose any maximizer $t_\star\in M$. Then $t_\star>0$ is fixed and $m_1(t_\star)=1/2$, so the polynomial part of $R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t_\star})$ is $O(n^{-1/2})$, while the variance term is $O(n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2})$. Therefore
\[
R_n^*\le R_n(\lambda_0n^{-t_\star})\le C_{\log} n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
Combining the last two displays gives
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
This completes all four rate regimes.

lem:phase-boundary-from-critical-exponen.proof_tex:
Set
\[
B=\min\{1/2,\ a_1+a_e,\ a_0+a_e\}.
\]
For each $\kappa>0$ and $t\ge 0$, let $h_\kappa(t)$ denote the pointwise minimum appearing in Definition `def:phase-exponent`, so that
\[

u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)=\sup_{t\ge 0}h_\kappa(t).
\]
We first show $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le B$ for every $\kappa>0$. For every $t\ge 0$, the entry $a_1+a_e-t$ in the minimum is at most $a_1+a_e$, the entry $a_0+a_e$ is exactly $a_0+a_e$, and the variance entry is at most $1/2$ (it is $1/2-t(1-\kappa)/2\le 1/2$ when $0<\kappa<1$, and it is exactly $1/2$ when $\kappa\ge 1$). Hence
\[
h_\kappa(t)\le B\qquad	ext{for all }t\ge 0,
\]
and taking the supremum over $t$ gives
\[

u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le B.
\]

Next we show that $\kappa\mapsto 
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)$ is nondecreasing and continuous. For fixed $t$, the two entries of $h_\kappa(t)$ that depend on $\kappa$ are
\[
\phi_1(\kappa,t)= egin{cases} rac12- rac{1-\kappa}{2}t,&0<\kappa<1,\  rac12,&\kappa\ge 1,\end{cases}\qquad \phi_2(\kappa,t)=a_1+ rac{\kappa t}{2}.
\]
Both are continuous and nondecreasing in $\kappa$, and the remaining entries of $h_\kappa(t)$ do not depend on $\kappa$. Therefore $h_\kappa(t)$ itself is continuous and nondecreasing in $\kappa$ for each fixed $t$. Since $h_\kappa(t)\le a_1+a_e-t$, any maximizer must lie in $[0,a_1+a_e]$; beyond that interval the term $a_1+a_e-t$ is negative, while $h_\kappa(0)=\min\{1/2,a_1,1/2+a_1,1/2+a_e,a_1+a_e,a_0+a_e\}>0$. Thus
\[

u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)=\max_{0\le t\le a_1+a_e} h_\kappa(t).
\]
Because $h_\kappa(t)$ is jointly continuous in $(\kappa,t)$ on every compact subset of $(0,\infty)	imes[0,a_1+a_e]$, the maximum theorem implies that $\kappa\mapsto 
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)$ is continuous. Monotonicity of each $h_\kappa(t)$ and of the supremum implies that $
u$ is nondecreasing.

Let
\[
E=\{\kappa>0: 
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)=B\}.
\]
By the continuity of $
u$, the set $E$ is closed. By the monotonicity of $
u$ and the bound $
u\le B$, the set $E$ is an upper set: if $\kappa\in E$ and $u\ge \kappa$, then
\[
B=
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\le 
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,u)\le B,
\]
so $u\in E$. Therefore $E$ is a closed upper ray. Definition `def:critical-exponent` sets $\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)=\inf E$, so necessarily
\[
E=[\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e),\infty).
\]
Consequently
\[

u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)<B\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e).
\]
This is the claimed benchmark boundary.

snapshot.stmt: Let B = min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e}. Then nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa) <= B for every kappa > 0. The map kappa -> nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa) is continuous and nondecreasing. Consequently the equality set {kappa > 0 : nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa) = B} is the upper ray [kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e), infinity), and therefore nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,kappa) < B exactly when kappa < kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e).

defs.def:critical-exponent: kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e) = inf{ kappa > 0 : nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e} }, where nu is the corrected phase exponent from def:phase-exponent

thm:clipped-upper.proof_tex:
Fix a deterministic clipping level $\lambda \in \Lambda_n$ and a law $P$ satisfying `ass:consistency`, `ass:ignorability`, `ass:right-overlap`, `ass:resid-treated`, `ass:resid-control`, `ass:estimable-g1`, `ass:estimable-g0`, and `ass:estimable-e`. Choose cross-fitted witness estimators attaining the stated rates, and write
+\[
+\delta_1=\hat g_{1n}-g_1,\qquad \delta_0=\hat g_{0n}-g_0,\qquad \hat e_{+,n}(x)=\min\{\hat e_n(x),1-\eta_+/2\}.
+\]
+By leave-one-fold-out cross-fitting, for each validation observation the fitted nuisance functions are measurable with respect to the training folds and independent of that validation draw. We may therefore condition on the training folds and treat the fitted nuisance functions as fixed when analyzing one validation term; the resulting bounds are then unconditional as well.
+
+First, Lemma `lem:oracle-clipped-score-unbiased` gives
+\[
+E_P[\psi_\lambda(O;g_0,g_1,e)]=\tau.
+\]
+
+Next decompose the estimation error as
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-\tau
+=\bigl(\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda\bigr)
++P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)
++\bigl(P\psi_\lambda-\tau\bigr).
+\]
+The last term is exactly zero by the unbiasedness identity above.
+
+For the drift term, Lemma `lem:feasible-score-drift-decomposition` yields
+\[
+|P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)|
+\le \pi(\lambda)^{1/2}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\lambda^{-1}\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}
++\frac{2}{\eta_+}\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}.
+\]
+Because the chosen witnesses attain the rates in `ass:estimable-g1`, `ass:estimable-g0`, and `ass:estimable-e`,
+\[
+\|\delta_1\|_{L_2(P_X)}=O_P(r_{1n}),\qquad
+\|\delta_0\|_{L_2(P_X)}=O_P(r_{0n}),\qquad
+\|\hat e_n-e\|_{L_2(P_X)}=O_P(r_{en}).
+\]
+Substituting these three bounds into the previous display gives
+\[
+P(\hat\psi_\lambda-\psi_\lambda)
+=O_P\bigl(\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}\bigr),
+\]
+where the constant factor $2/\eta_+$ is absorbed into the $O_P(\cdot)$ notation.
+
+For the empirical-process term, Lemma `lem:feasible-score-fluctuation-bound` gives
+\[
+\hat\tau_\lambda-P\hat\psi_\lambda
+=O_P\!\Bigl(n^{-1/2}\bigl[E\{e_\lambda(X)^{-1}\}^{1/2}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en}\bigr]\Bigr).
+\]
+
+Combining the last two displays with the exact equality $P\psi_\lambda=\tau$, we obtain
+\[
+|\hat\tau_\lambda-\tau|
+=O_P\!\Bigl(n^{-1/2}E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}
++n^{-1/2}(\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en})
++\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}
++\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}
++r_{0n}r_{en}\Bigr).
+\]
+By Definition `def:exact-frontier-functional`, the right-hand side is exactly $U_n(\lambda)$. Hence
+\[
+|\hat\tau_\lambda-\tau|=O_P\bigl(U_n(\lambda)\bigr).
+\]
+This proves the theorem. The parenthetical statement about $P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})$ or $P\in W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})$ is immediate because both classes are defined by imposing the displayed assumptions (and additional conditions not used in this proof).

snapshot.stmt: Choose cross-fitted witness estimators hat_g_1n, hat_g_0n, and hat_e_n attaining the rates in ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e. Then for every law P satisfying ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:right-overlap, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e, and in particular for every P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) or P in W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en), and every deterministic lambda in Lambda_n, E_P[psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e)] = tau and |hat_tau_lambda - tau| = O_P(U_n(lambda)).

defs.def:weak-overlap-class: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:tail-shell, ass:right-overlap, ass:region-compatibility, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }
defs.def:regular-overlap-class: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:strict-left-overlap-sanity, ass:right-overlap, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }
defs.def:oracle-clipped-score: psi_lambda(O; g_0, g_1, e) = g_1(X) - g_0(X) + D (Y - g_1(X)) / e_lambda(X) - (1 - D) (Y - g_0(X)) / (1 - e(X))
defs.def:plugin-score: hat_psi_lambda(O) = hat_g_1n(X) - hat_g_0n(X) + D (Y - hat_g_1n(X)) / hat_e_lambda(X) - (1 - D) (Y - hat_g_0n(X)) / (1 - min{hat_e_n(X), 1 - eta_plus / 2})
defs.def:crossfit-estimator: hat_tau_lambda = n^(-1) sum_{i=1}^n hat_psi_lambda(O_i; hat_g_0n^(-i), hat_g_1n^(-i), hat_e_n^(-i), eta_plus, lambda)
defs.def:exact-frontier-functional: U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + n^(-1/2) (lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + pi(lambda)^(1/2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

assumptions.ass:iid: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:consistency: Y = D Y(1) + (1 - D) Y(0) almost surely.
assumptions.ass:ignorability: (Y(1), Y(0)) is independent of D conditional on X.
assumptions.ass:resid-treated: sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_1(X))^2 | D = 1, X = x] <= sigma_1_sq for some finite sigma_1_sq.
assumptions.ass:resid-control: sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_0(X))^2 | D = 0, X = x] <= sigma_0_sq for some finite sigma_0_sq.
assumptions.ass:estimable-g1: The nuisance g_1 is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_1n from an i.i.d. sample of O.
assumptions.ass:estimable-g0: The nuisance g_0 is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_0n from an i.i.d. sample of O.
assumptions.ass:estimable-e: The nuisance e is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_en from an i.i.d. sample of O.

prop:tail-order-reduction.proof_tex:
Fix $P \in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})$ and $\lambda \in \Lambda_n$. By Definition `def:clipping-grid`, $0<\lambda\le \lambda_0$. For $j=0,1,2,\dots$ define the dyadic shells
\[
A_j(\lambda)=\{2^{-(j+1)}\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{-j}\lambda\}.
\]
These sets are disjoint and their union is $\{e(X)\le \lambda\}$. By `ass:tail-shell`, applied with upper endpoint $2^{-j}\lambda$,
\[
c_{m shell,-}2^{-j\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}\le P_X(A_j(\lambda))\le c_{m shell,+}2^{-j\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}\qquad (j\ge 0).
\]
Summing over $j$ gives
\[
\pi(\lambda)=P\{e(X)\le \lambda\}=\sum_{j\ge 0}P_X(A_j(\lambda)).
\]
Keeping only the first shell yields the lower bound
\[
\pi(\lambda)\ge P_X(A_0(\lambda))\ge c_{m shell,-}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]
Using the geometric series for the upper bound,
\[
\pi(\lambda)\le c_{m shell,+}\lambda^{\kappa}\sum_{j\ge 0}2^{-j\kappa}= rac{c_{m shell,+}}{1-2^{-\kappa}}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]
Hence there are constants $0<c_{\pi,1}\le c_{\pi,2}<\infty$, depending only on the shell constants and $\kappa$, such that
\[
c_{\pi,1}\lambda^{\kappa}\le \pi(\lambda)\le c_{\pi,2}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]

We next compare $E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]$ with $v_\kappa(\lambda)^2$. Choose the unique integer $J=J(\lambda)\ge 0$ such that
\[
2^J\lambda\le \lambda_0<2^{J+1}\lambda.
\]
Then $2^J\lambda>\lambda_0/2$. Decompose
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]=\lambda^{-1}\pi(\lambda)+\sum_{j=0}^{J-1}E\!\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}ight]+E\!\left[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{e(X)>2^J\lambda\}ight].
\]
For $0\le j\le J-1$, on the shell $\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}$ we have
\[
(2^{j+1}\lambda)^{-1}\le e(X)^{-1}\le (2^j\lambda)^{-1}.
\]
Applying `ass:tail-shell` with upper endpoint $2^{j+1}\lambda$ gives
\[
c_{m shell,-}2^{(j+1)\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}\le P\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}\le c_{m shell,+}2^{(j+1)\kappa}\lambda^{\kappa}.
\]
Therefore there exist constants $0<\underline C\le \overline C<\infty$, depending only on the shell constants and $\kappa$, such that
\[
\underline C\,\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}\le E\!\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}ight]\le \overline C\,\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}.
\]
For the remainder term, $e(X)>2^J\lambda>\lambda_0/2$ implies $e_\lambda(X)^{-1}\le 2/\lambda_0$, so
\[
0\le E\!\left[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{e(X)>2^J\lambda\}ight]\le 2/\lambda_0.
\]

If $0<\kappa<1$, then $\kappa-1<0$. The clipped piece already yields the lower bound
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\ge \lambda^{-1}\pi(\lambda)\ge c_{\pi,1}\lambda^{\kappa-1}.
\]
For the upper bound,
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{\pi,2}\lambda^{\kappa-1}+\overline C\lambda^{\kappa-1}\sum_{j=0}^{J-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}+2/\lambda_0.
\]
Because $\sum_{j\ge 0}2^{j(\kappa-1)}<\infty$ and $\lambda\le \lambda_0$ implies $2/\lambda_0\le C\lambda^{\kappa-1}$, there are constants $0<c_{e,1}\le c_{e,2}<\infty$ such that
\[
c_{e,1}\lambda^{\kappa-1}\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{e,2}\lambda^{\kappa-1}.
\]
Since $v_\kappa(\lambda)^2=\lambda^{-(1-\kappa)}=\lambda^{\kappa-1}$ in this regime,
\[
c_{e,1}v_\kappa(\lambda)^2\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{e,2}v_\kappa(\lambda)^2.
\]

If $\kappa=1$, each shell contributes constant order. Indeed the previous display becomes
\[
\underline C\le E\!\left[e(X)^{-1}\mathbf 1\{2^j\lambda<e(X)\le 2^{j+1}\lambda\}ight]\le \overline C\qquad (0\le j\le J-1).
\]
Hence
\[
\underline C J\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{\pi,2}+\overline C J+2/\lambda_0.
\]
Since $2^J\lambda\le \lambda_0<2^{J+1}\lambda$,
\[
J\le \log_2(\lambda_0/\lambda)<J+1.
\]
Thus $J+1$ is comparable to $1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)$, and so there are constants $0<c_{e,1}\le c_{e,2}<\infty$ with
\[
c_{e,1}(1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda))\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{e,2}(1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)).
\]
Because $v_1(\lambda)^2=1+\log(\lambda_0/\lambda)$, this is exactly
\[
c_{e,1}v_1(\lambda)^2\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{e,2}v_1(\lambda)^2.
\]

If $\kappa>1$, let $A_*=\{\lambda_0/2<e(X)\le \lambda_0\}$. On $A_*$ we have $e_\lambda(X)=\max\{e(X),\lambda\}\le \lambda_0$, because both $e(X)$ and $\lambda$ are at most $\lambda_0$. Therefore
\[
E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\ge \lambda_0^{-1}P(A_*)\ge c_{m shell,-}\lambda_0^{\kappa-1}.
\]
For the upper bound, the clipped piece satisfies $\lambda^{-1}\pi(\lambda)\le c_{\pi,2}\lambda^{\kappa-1}\le c_{\pi,2}\lambda_0^{\kappa-1}$. Also
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{J-1}\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{j(\kappa-1)}\le C\lambda^{\kappa-1}2^{J(\kappa-1)}\le C\lambda_0^{\kappa-1},
\]
because $2^J\lambda\le \lambda_0$. Together with the remainder bound $2/\lambda_0$, this shows that there are constants $0<c_{e,1}\le c_{e,2}<\infty$ such that
\[
c_{e,1}\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{e,2}.
\]
Since $v_\kappa(\lambda)^2=1$ for $\kappa>1$, this is again
\[
c_{e,1}v_\kappa(\lambda)^2\le E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]\le c_{e,2}v_\kappa(\lambda)^2.
\]

Finally, compare $U_n(\lambda)$ and $R_n(\lambda)$. By `def:exact-frontier-functional`,
\[
U_n(\lambda)=n^{-1/2}E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}+n^{-1/2}(\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en})+\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
By `def:frontier-functional`,
\[
R_n(\lambda)=n^{-1/2}v_\kappa(\lambda)+n^{-1/2}(\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en})+\lambda^{\kappa/2}r_{1n}+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
The middle four summands and the last two summands are identical in the two displays. The first term is comparable because $E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]$ is comparable to $v_\kappa(\lambda)^2$, hence $E[e_\lambda(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}$ is comparable to $v_\kappa(\lambda)$. The third term is comparable because $\pi(\lambda)$ is comparable to $\lambda^{\kappa}$, hence $\pi(\lambda)^{1/2}$ is comparable to $\lambda^{\kappa/2}$. Since all summands are nonnegative, taking the minimum of the lower comparison constants and the maximum of the upper comparison constants yields $0<c_1\le c_2<\infty$ such that
\[
c_1R_n(\lambda)\le U_n(\lambda)\le c_2R_n(\lambda).
\]
This proves all three claimed comparisons.

snapshot.stmt: For every P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) and every lambda in Lambda_n there exist constants 0 < c_1 <= c_2 < infinity, depending only on the class constants, such that c_1 lambda^kappa <= pi(lambda) <= c_2 lambda^kappa, c_1 v_kappa(lambda)^2 <= E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)] <= c_2 v_kappa(lambda)^2, and c_1 R_n(lambda) <= U_n(lambda) <= c_2 R_n(lambda).

defs.def:weak-overlap-class: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:tail-shell, ass:right-overlap, ass:region-compatibility, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }
defs.def:variance-scale: v_kappa(lambda) = lambda^(-(1 - kappa) / 2) if 0 < kappa < 1, v_kappa(lambda) = (1 + log(lambda_0 / lambda))^(1/2) if kappa = 1, and v_kappa(lambda) = 1 if kappa > 1
defs.def:exact-frontier-functional: U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + n^(-1/2) (lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + pi(lambda)^(1/2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en
defs.def:frontier-functional: R_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) v_kappa(lambda) + n^(-1/2) (lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + lambda^(kappa / 2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

assumptions.ass:tail-shell: There exist c_shell_minus > 0, c_shell_plus > 0, and lambda_0 in (0,1) such that c_shell_minus lambda^kappa <= P(lambda / 2 < e(X) <= lambda) <= c_shell_plus lambda^kappa for every lambda in (0, lambda_0].

prop:strict-overlap-sanity.proof_tex:
Fix $P\in W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})$ and $\lambda\in\Lambda_n$ with $\lambda<\eta_-$. By `ass:strict-left-overlap-sanity`, $e(X)\ge \eta_-$ almost surely. Hence $e(x)>\lambda$ for every $x$, and therefore Definition `def:true-clipped-propensity` gives
\[
e_\lambda(x)=\max\{e(x),\lambda\}=e(x)\qquad	ext{for all }x\in\mathcal X.
\]
Also, by Definition `def:tail-mass-functional`,
\[
\pi(\lambda)=P\{e(X)\le \lambda\}=0,
\]
because the event $\{e(X)\le \lambda\}$ is empty almost surely. Substituting these identities into Definition `def:exact-frontier-functional` yields
\[
U_n(\lambda)=n^{-1/2}E[e(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}+n^{-1/2}(\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en})+\lambda^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
This is the displayed simplification.

Now assume $\lambda_0<\eta_-$. Then the grid point $\lambda=\lambda_0$ belongs to $\Lambda_n$ by Definition `def:clipping-grid` with $t=0$. Because every law in $W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})$ satisfies the assumptions of `thm:clipped-upper`, that theorem gives
\[
|\hat	au_{\lambda_0}-	au|=O_P(U_n(\lambda_0)).
\]
This is exactly the asserted in-particular conclusion.

snapshot.stmt: For every P in W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en), if lambda in Lambda_n satisfies lambda < eta_minus, then e_lambda(x) = e(x) for all x in X and pi(lambda) = 0, so U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + n^(-1/2)(lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en. In particular, if lambda_0 < eta_minus then choosing the fixed grid point lambda = lambda_0 yields |hat_tau_lambda - tau| = O_P(U_n(lambda_0)).

defs.def:regular-overlap-class: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:strict-left-overlap-sanity, ass:right-overlap, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }
defs.def:exact-frontier-functional: U_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) (E[e_lambda(X)^(-1)])^(1/2) + n^(-1/2) (lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + pi(lambda)^(1/2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

conj:tail-lower-bound.proof_tex:
Let \((s_0,t_0)\) and \((s_1,t_1)\) be a pair attaining the parameter diameter in the displayed hypothesis, and write
\[
P_0=P_{s_0,t_0},\qquad P_1=P_{s_1,t_1},\qquad \Delta=|\tau(P_1)-\tau(P_0)|.
\]
Then \(\Delta\ge c_0R_n^*\). By the pairwise chi-square hypothesis,
\[
\chi^2(P_0^{\otimes n},P_1^{\otimes n})\le C_{\chi}<4.
\]
Hence total variation is bounded by
\[
\operatorname{TV}(P_0^{\otimes n},P_1^{\otimes n})
\le \tfrac12\chi^2(P_0^{\otimes n},P_1^{\otimes n})^{1/2}
\le \frac{\sqrt{C_{\chi}}}{2}<1.
\]
Now apply Lemma `lem:absolute-risk-two-point`. For every estimator \(\hat\tau\),
\[
\max\Bigl\{E_{P_0}|\hat\tau-\tau(P_0)|,\ E_{P_1}|\hat\tau-\tau(P_1)|\Bigr\}
\ge \frac{\Delta}{4}\Bigl(1-\operatorname{TV}(P_0^{\otimes n},P_1^{\otimes n})\Bigr)
\ge \frac{c_0}{4}\Bigl(1-\frac{\sqrt{C_{\chi}}}{2}\Bigr)R_n^*.
\]
Because \(\{P_{s,t}\}\subset W_n^{\rm lb}(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})\),
\[
\sup_{P\in W_n^{\rm lb}(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge \max\Bigl\{E_{P_0}|\hat\tau-\tau(P_0)|,\ E_{P_1}|\hat\tau-\tau(P_1)|\Bigr\}.
\]
Taking the infimum over all estimators proves
\[
\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in W_n^{\rm lb}(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge cR_n^*,
\]
with
\[
c=\frac{c_0}{4}\Bigl(1-\frac{\sqrt{C_{\chi}}}{2}\Bigr)>0.
\]
This is the claimed lower bound.

snapshot.stmt: Let lambda_dagger_n be any deterministic selection satisfying def:near-optimal-threshold. Suppose the handle H_{n,lambda_dagger_n} is completed into a four-point family {P_{s,t} : (s,t) in {-1,1}^2} contained in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) such that the pairwise chi-square distances between the n-sample product laws P_{s,t}^{\otimes n} are bounded by a sufficiently small universal constant C_chi < 4 and the parameter diameter satisfies max_{(s,t),(s',t')} |tau(P_{s,t}) - tau(P_{s',t'})| >= c_0 R_n^* for some universal c_0 > 0. Then inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= c R_n^* for some universal c > 0.

defs.def:lower-bound-subclass: { P : P satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:tail-shell, ass:right-overlap, ass:region-compatibility, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, ass:estimable-e, and ass:regular-cell-mass at sample size n }
defs.def:near-optimal-threshold: lambda_dagger_n in { lambda in Lambda_n : R_n(lambda) <= 2 R_n^* }
defs.def:tail-shell: S_lambda = { x in X : lambda / 2 < e(x) <= lambda }
defs.def:regular-cell: B_lambda = { x in X : 2 lambda < e(x) <= 1 - eta_plus }
defs.def:least-favorable-family: Handle for a lower-bound family: start from a base law P in W_n^lb(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en); for each (s,t) in {-1,1}^2 define g_{1,s,t}(x) = g_1(x) + s delta_1n(lambda) 1_{S_lambda}(x), g_{0,s,t}(x) = g_0(x) + t delta_0n(lambda) 1_{B_lambda}(x), h_{s,t,lambda}(x) = s min{u_1n(lambda), log((1 - eta_plus / 2) / (eta_plus / 2)) - log(e(x) / (1 - e(x)))} 1_{S_lambda}(x) + t min{u_0n(lambda), log((1 - eta_plus / 2) / (eta_plus / 2)) - log(e(x) / (1 - e(x)))} 1_{B_lambda}(x), and e_{s,t}(x) = expit(log(e(x) / (1 - e(x))) + h_{s,t,lambda}(x)). This clipping map keeps e_{s,t}(x) <= 1 - eta_plus / 2 pointwise; the remaining open task is to verify bounded pairwise chi-square distance while keeping P_X and the centered residual laws fixed.

assumptions.ass:iid: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:consistency: Y = D Y(1) + (1 - D) Y(0) almost surely.
assumptions.ass:ignorability: (Y(1), Y(0)) is independent of D conditional on X.
assumptions.ass:tail-shell: There exist c_shell_minus > 0, c_shell_plus > 0, and lambda_0 in (0,1) such that c_shell_minus lambda^kappa <= P(lambda / 2 < e(X) <= lambda) <= c_shell_plus lambda^kappa for every lambda in (0, lambda_0].
assumptions.ass:resid-treated: sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_1(X))^2 | D = 1, X = x] <= sigma_1_sq for some finite sigma_1_sq.
assumptions.ass:resid-control: sup_{x in X} E[(Y - g_0(X))^2 | D = 0, X = x] <= sigma_0_sq for some finite sigma_0_sq.
assumptions.ass:estimable-g1: The nuisance g_1 is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_1n from an i.i.d. sample of O.
assumptions.ass:estimable-g0: The nuisance g_0 is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_0n from an i.i.d. sample of O.
assumptions.ass:estimable-e: The nuisance e is L2(P_X)-estimable at rate r_en from an i.i.d. sample of O.
assumptions.ass:regular-cell-mass: There exists c_reg > 0 such that P(2 lambda_0 < e(X) <= 1 - eta_plus) >= c_reg.

conj:minimax-frontier.proof_tex:
For the lower bound, note first that `def:lower-bound-subclass` is obtained from `def:weak-overlap-class` by adding the extra member property `ass:regular-cell-mass` and keeping all other defining properties unchanged. Hence
\[
W_n^{\mathrm{lb}}(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})\subseteq W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en}).
\]
Therefore, for every estimator $\hat	au$,
\[
\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|\ge \sup_{P\in W_n^{\mathrm{lb}}(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|.
\]
Taking the infimum over estimators preserves the inequality, so
\[
\inf_{\hat	au}\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|\ge \inf_{\hat	au}\sup_{P\in W_n^{\mathrm{lb}}(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|.
\]
Now apply `conj:tail-lower-bound`, which yields a universal constant $c_{\mathrm{lb}}>0$ such that the right-hand side is at least $c_{\mathrm{lb}}R_n^*$. Hence
\[
\inf_{\hat	au}\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|\ge c_{\mathrm{lb}}R_n^*.
\]
This proves the first sentence.

For the second sentence, assume there exist a universal constant $C>0$ and a deterministic sequence $\lambda_n\in\Lambda_n$ such that $R_n(\lambda_n)\le 2R_n^*$ and
\[
\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au_{\lambda_n}-	au(P)|\le C U_n(\lambda_n).
\]
Because the minimax risk is the infimum over all estimators, it is bounded above by the risk of this particular estimator: 
\[
\inf_{\hat	au}\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|\le \sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au_{\lambda_n}-	au(P)|\le C U_n(\lambda_n).
\]
By `prop:tail-order-reduction`, there is a universal comparison constant $c_2<\infty$ such that $U_n(\lambda_n)\le c_2R_n(\lambda_n)$. Therefore
\[
\inf_{\hat	au}\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|\le Cc_2R_n(\lambda_n)\le 2Cc_2R_n^*.
\]
Combining this upper bound with the previously proved lower bound gives
\[
c_{\mathrm{lb}}R_n^*\le \inf_{\hat	au}\sup_{P\in W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})}E_P|\hat	au-	au(P)|\le 2Cc_2R_n^*.
\]
Thus the minimax absolute risk over $W_n(\kappa,r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})$ is equivalent, up to universal constants, to $R_n^*$.

snapshot.stmt: There exists a universal constant c > 0 such that inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| >= c R_n^*. Moreover, if the upper theorem is strengthened to a uniform absolute-risk bound, namely if there exist a universal constant C > 0 and a deterministic sequence lambda_n in Lambda_n with R_n(lambda_n) <= 2 R_n^* such that sup_{P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en)} E_P |hat_tau_{lambda_n} - tau(P)| <= C U_n(lambda_n), then the minimax absolute risk over W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) is equivalent, up to universal constants, to R_n^*.

defs.def:weak-overlap-class: { P : P admits potential outcomes (Y(1), Y(0)) and satisfies ass:consistency, ass:ignorability, ass:tail-shell, ass:right-overlap, ass:region-compatibility, ass:resid-treated, ass:resid-control, ass:estimable-g1, ass:estimable-g0, and ass:estimable-e at sample size n }
defs.def:frontier-functional: R_n(lambda) = n^(-1/2) v_kappa(lambda) + n^(-1/2) (lambda^(-1) r_1n + r_0n + lambda^(-3/2) r_en + r_en) + lambda^(kappa / 2) r_1n + lambda^(-1) r_1n r_en + r_0n r_en

prop:phase-diagram.proof_tex:
Specialize the nuisance-rate sequences to $r_{0n}=n^{-a_0}$, $r_{1n}=n^{-a_1}$, and $r_{en}=n^{-a_e}$. Under this specialization, the weak-overlap class in `conj:minimax-frontier` is exactly the rate-specialized class $E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)$, and the quantity $R_n^*$ is the corresponding specialized frontier. The second sentence of `conj:minimax-frontier` says that, once its stated uniform absolute-risk upper bound is available, the conjectured minimax absolute risk is equivalent up to constants to this specialized $R_n^*$. Thus the proposition reduces to computing the asymptotic order of $R_n^*$.

If $\kappa
eq 1$, Lemma `lem:frontier-rate-specialization` gives
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Hence the conjectured minimax absolute rate over $E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)$ is $n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}$.

If $\kappa=1$ and $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)<1/2$, the same lemma gives
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)},
\]
so the conjectured minimax absolute rate is $n^{-
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)}$.

If $\kappa=1$ and $
u(a_0,a_1,a_e,1)=1/2$, Lemma `lem:frontier-rate-specialization` separates the two boundary subcases. When $t=0$ attains the supremum in `def:phase-exponent`, the fixed threshold $\lambda=\lambda_0$ already attains the optimal power exponent, and
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-1/2}.
\]
When every maximizer is strictly positive, every near-optimal threshold shrinks polynomially, the variance channel contributes the additional logarithmic factor, and
\[
R_n^*\asymp n^{-1/2}(\log n)^{1/2}.
\]
These are exactly the two $\kappa=1$ cases stated in the proposition.

For the final sentence, set $B=\min\{1/2,a_1+a_e,a_0+a_e\}$. Lemma `lem:phase-boundary-from-critical-exponent` shows that
\[

u(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)<B\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e).
\]
Therefore the polynomial bounded-overlap benchmark exponent $B$ is strictly deteriorated exactly on the region $\kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)$. The preceding $\kappa=1$ clause records the remaining logarithmic boundary effect when the power exponent still equals $1/2$ but every optimal threshold shrinks.

snapshot.stmt: After correcting def:frontier-functional, def:phase-exponent, and def:critical-exponent as proposed below, the conjectured minimax absolute rate over E_n(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) is n^(-nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa)) when kappa != 1. When kappa = 1, the conjectured rate is n^(-nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1)) if nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1) < 1/2. If nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, 1) = 1/2, then the rate is n^(-1/2) when the fixed-threshold choice t = 0 attains the supremum in def:phase-exponent, and it is n^(-1/2) (log n)^(1/2) when every maximizer of def:phase-exponent is strictly positive. One-sided overlap decay strictly changes the bounded-overlap structure-agnostic benchmark min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e} exactly when kappa < kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e).

defs.def:rate-exponent-subclass: { P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) : r_0n = n^(-a_0), r_1n = n^(-a_1), and r_en = n^(-a_e) }
defs.def:phase-exponent: nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = sup_{t >= 0} min{ 1/2 - t (1 - kappa) / 2, a_1 + t kappa / 2, 1/2 + a_1 - t, 1/2 + a_e - 3 t / 2, a_1 + a_e - t, a_0 + a_e } if 0 < kappa < 1, and nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = sup_{t >= 0} min{ 1/2, a_1 + t kappa / 2, 1/2 + a_1 - t, 1/2 + a_e - 3 t / 2, a_1 + a_e - t, a_0 + a_e } if kappa >= 1
defs.def:critical-exponent: kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e) = inf{ kappa > 0 : nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e} }, where nu is the corrected phase exponent from def:phase-exponent

prop:comparator-separation.proof_tex:
Let
\[
B:=\min\{1/2,\ a_1+a_e,\ a_0+a_e\}.
\]
We prove the two assertions separately.

For the regular-overlap comparator, fix any law \(P\in E_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(a_0,a_1,a_e)\) and assume \(\lambda_0<\eta_-\). By Definition `def:regular-rate-exponent-subclass`, the law \(P\) belongs to \(W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}(r_{0n},r_{1n},r_{en})\) and its nuisance-rate sequences satisfy
\[
r_{0n}=n^{-a_0},\qquad r_{1n}=n^{-a_1},\qquad r_{en}=n^{-a_e}.
\]
Because \(P\in W_n^{\mathrm{reg}}\), the strict-left-overlap condition holds: \(e(X)\ge \eta_-\) almost surely. Since \(\lambda_0<\eta_-\), Proposition `prop:strict-overlap-sanity` applies at the fixed grid point \(\lambda=\lambda_0\) and yields
\[
|\hat\tau_{\lambda_0}-\tau|=O_P\bigl(U_n(\lambda_0)\bigr),
\]
with
\[
U_n(\lambda_0)
=
 n^{-1/2}E[e(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}
 + n^{-1/2}\bigl(\lambda_0^{-1}r_{1n}+r_{0n}+\lambda_0^{-3/2}r_{en}+r_{en}\bigr)
 + \lambda_0^{-1}r_{1n}r_{en}+r_{0n}r_{en}.
\]
Under strict left overlap, \(e(X)^{-1}\le \eta_-^{-1}\) almost surely, so \(E[e(X)^{-1}]^{1/2}\le \eta_-^{-1/2}\), which is a finite constant independent of \(n\). Substituting the rate specialization gives
\[
U_n(\lambda_0)
=
O\Bigl(
 n^{-1/2}
 + n^{-(1/2+a_1)}
 + n^{-(1/2+a_0)}
 + n^{-(1/2+a_e)}
 + n^{-(a_1+a_e)}
 + n^{-(a_0+a_e)}
\Bigr).
\]
Since \(a_0,a_1,a_e>0\), the three middle empirical-process terms have exponents strictly larger than \(1/2\), hence each is \(O(n^{-1/2})\). Therefore
\[
U_n(\lambda_0)=O\bigl(n^{-1/2}+n^{-(a_1+a_e)}+n^{-(a_0+a_e)}\bigr).
\]
Because \(B=\min\{1/2,a_1+a_e,a_0+a_e\}\), each summand on the right-hand side is \(O(n^{-B})\). Hence
\[
U_n(\lambda_0)=O(n^{-B}),
\]
and therefore
\[
|\hat\tau_{\lambda_0}-\tau|=O_P(n^{-B}).
\]
This proves the first sentence.

For the weak-overlap class, assume instead that \(0<\kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)\). Proposition `prop:phase-diagram` states that one-sided overlap decay strictly changes the bounded-overlap benchmark exponent \(B\) exactly on the region \(\kappa<\kappa^*(a_0,a_1,a_e)\). Equivalently, on this region the conjectured minimax absolute rate over \(E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)\) is strictly slower than \(n^{-B}\). Writing that conjectured minimax rate as \(n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}\), the last sentence of `prop:phase-diagram` gives
\[
\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)<B.
\]
Because `prop:phase-diagram` is itself derived from `conj:minimax-frontier`, this means that there exists a constant \(c>0\), independent of \(n\), such that for all large \(n\),
\[
\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge c\,n^{-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Multiplying by \(n^B\) yields
\[
n^B\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|
\ge c\,n^{B-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}.
\]
Since \(B-\nu(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)>0\), the right-hand side diverges to \(+\infty\). Therefore
\[
n^{\min\{1/2,a_1+a_e,a_0+a_e\}}\inf_{\hat\tau}\sup_{P\in E_n(a_0,a_1,a_e,\kappa)}E_P|\hat\tau-\tau(P)|\to\infty.
\]
This proves the second sentence.

snapshot.stmt: Assume conj:minimax-frontier. On E_n^reg(a_0, a_1, a_e), if lambda_0 < eta_minus then prop:strict-overlap-sanity yields an estimator whose estimation error satisfies |hat_tau_{lambda_0} - tau| = O_P(n^(-min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e})). If instead 0 < kappa < kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e), then n^(min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e}) inf_{hat_tau} sup_{P in E_n(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa)} E_P |hat_tau - tau(P)| -> infinity.

defs.def:rate-exponent-subclass: { P in W_n(kappa, r_0n, r_1n, r_en) : r_0n = n^(-a_0), r_1n = n^(-a_1), and r_en = n^(-a_e) }
defs.def:regular-rate-exponent-subclass: { P in W_n^reg(r_0n, r_1n, r_en) : r_0n = n^(-a_0), r_1n = n^(-a_1), and r_en = n^(-a_e) }
defs.def:critical-exponent: kappa^*(a_0, a_1, a_e) = inf{ kappa > 0 : nu(a_0, a_1, a_e, kappa) = min{1/2, a_1 + a_e, a_0 + a_e} }, where nu is the corrected phase exponent from def:phase-exponent
